articles.title: Like Humans, Baboons Get Jealous of Their Siblings
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Like Humans, Baboons Get Jealous of Their Siblings
A new study found that young baboons try to interrupt their mother when she is grooming a sibling
If you have siblings, you may remember fighting...
articles.content:
Like Humans, Baboons Get Jealous of Their Siblings
A new study found that young baboons try to interrupt their mother when she is grooming a sibling
If you have siblings, you may remember fighting for your parents’ attention when you were younger. It turns out sibling rivalry isn’t just something that affects humans. A new study has found that young baboons try to steal their mothers away from their brothers or sisters.
Scientists observed baboon families in Namibia between August and December 2021, and noticed that when a mother was grooming a child, a sibling—often an older one—would try to interrupt them. The baboon vying for parental attention would bite, slap, cry and sometimes even lure their sibling away in order to supplant their place in their mother’s arms.
Because baboons usually have one baby every 18 to 24 months, “it was generally thought there was no real competition between siblings, because brothers and sisters are different ages and do not necessarily need their mother and her resources at the same time,” explains Axelle Delaunay, a behavioral ecologist at the University of Turku in Finland and the study’s first author, to Agence France-Presse.
However, sibling interference in baboons “strikingly mirrors patterns of sibling jealousy reported in humans,” Delaunay and her colleagues write in their study, published in the journal Proceedings of the Royal Society B on February 11.
“It’s totally relatable,” says Joan Silk, a primatologist at Arizona State University who was not involved in the work, to Annie Roth at the New York Times. “I have two sisters and two children, so I can relate, but the fact that it’s relatable doesn’t always mean it’s true.”
In this case, it likely is. “This group of researchers has really pushed the envelope about what we know about relationships between individuals,” Silk tells the Times.
Overall, Delaunay and her team found that baboons are more likely to interfere when their mother is grooming a sibling than when she is resting and already free. Offspring were also twice as likely to interfere against their younger siblings, and targeted siblings of the same sex more often. Interference was most common between brothers, they found.
Female baboons also have “favorite” children that they groom disproportionately more than others. The researchers found that the baboons target that favorite child, lending more evidence towards the idea that the interrupting behavior is motivated by jealousy. “Our findings further indicate that baboons are aware of their own maternal bond relative to those of their siblings,” the authors write in the study, though further studies are needed to determine the cognitive complexity of these skills.
And good news for the mothers: the jealous behavior faded away as the baboon got older.
Delaunay and her team aren’t sure what benefits these displays of sibling rivalry have for the jealous offspring. While sibling interference interrupted bouts of grooming in 19 percent of instances, it was only successful in getting the interrupter access to grooming by their mother in 9 percent of cases. “Most of the time, interference doesn’t really work,” Delaunay tells the Times. “So what are the benefits?”
She hopes that future research will answer that question, and that more scientists will study primate emotions. “While we can’t ask them how they feel, we know that emotions provoke physiological changes, behavioral changes, some cognitive changes—and this you can measure,” she adds.

articles.title: Why Do Humans Have Chins? They Might Be an Evolutionary Accident, New Research Suggests
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Why Do Humans Have Chins? They Might Be an Evolutionary Accident, New Research Suggests
The bony facial protrusion might be an evolutionary byproduct that resulted from changes to other parts of the...
articles.content:
Why Do Humans Have Chins? They Might Be an Evolutionary Accident, New Research Suggests
The bony facial protrusion might be an evolutionary byproduct that resulted from changes to other parts of the skull, according to a new study
Why do humans have chins? Scientists have long puzzled over this question, since no other living primate species boasts this bony facial protrusion and chins serve no apparent purpose. Even our extinct close relatives like Neanderthals and Denisovans lacked chins, sporting flat or receding lower jaws instead.
Researchers have come up with numerous hypotheses to help explain the evolution of this unique trait, which is so distinctive that anthropologists often use it to positively identify Homo sapiens fossils. Maybe chins arose from sexual selection, because Homo sapiens were attracted to mates with lower jaw projections for some reason. Perhaps they played an important role in our ability to chew food or talk. Or, possibly, chins developed to help protect the delicate throat and neck from injury.
Now, new research published January 29 in the journal PLOS One makes the case for another previously proposed explanation. Chins probably didn’t develop for a specific reason but, rather, came about incidentally because of other evolutionary changes to the body.
Scientists call these types of byproduct traits “spandrels,” a term they borrowed from the field of architecture, where it refers to the purposeless triangular spaces created by the construction of adjacent arches in buildings. Other examples of what are likely biological spandrels include the human belly button, the shoulder hump of the extinct giant Irish deer and the male-mimicking genitalia of female hyenas.
Fun fact: Chin fake out
Elephants have chin-like protrusions from losing the lower jaw’s front teeth and having a fleshy lower lip that anchors the trunk, which is a fusion of the nose and upper lip. That has left something that resembles a chin—but isn’t actually one.
“The chin evolved largely by accident and not through direct selection, but as an evolutionary byproduct resulting from direct selection on other parts of the skull,” says study co-author Noreen von Cramon-Taubadel, an anthropologist at the University of Buffalo, in a statement.
To unravel the enduring mystery of the human chin, scientists studied 532 skulls kept in museum collections in Europe and the United States. Some of the skulls belonged to humans, while others were from chimpanzees, gorillas, gibbons, bonobos, orangutans and other types of apes.
On each specimen, the researchers examined 32 landmarks and 46 distances between those various features, then mapped the data onto an evolutionary family tree. Next, they ran computer simulations involving genetics to understand how differences between the related species might have arisen.
The analysis revealed that nine traits of the lower jawbone were related to the evolution of the chin. Of those, three appear to have resulted from direct evolutionary selection, meaning they emerged because they boosted Homo sapiens’ chances of survival and reproduction somehow. The remaining six, however, seem to have resulted from random evolutionary chance or as a byproduct of other traits. This evidence, the study authors write, aligns best with the idea that chins are spandrels.
More than likely, their analysis suggests, chins arose as a byproduct of other adaptations—probably, a combination of humans evolving an upright posture, larger heads and smaller teeth and jaws.
“Just because an observable feature like the chin looks like a distinct ‘thing’ doesn’t mean it actually evolves as an independent unit,” says James DiFrisco, a biologist at the Francis Crick Institute in England who was not involved with the new research, tells New Scientist’s Christa Lesté-Lasserre.

articles.title: This Luxury Steamer Disappeared on a Stormy Night in 1872. Nearly 150 Years to the Day, It Was Found at the Bottom of Lake Michigan
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
This Luxury Steamer Disappeared on a Stormy Night in 1872. Nearly 150 Years to the Day, It Was Found at the Bottom of Lake Michigan
The “Lac La Belle” was discovered 20 miles off the coast of...
articles.content:
This Luxury Steamer Disappeared on a Stormy Night in 1872. Nearly 150 Years to the Day, It Was Found at the Bottom of Lake Michigan
The “Lac La Belle” was discovered 20 miles off the coast of Wisconsin in 2022 after a fisherman offered shipwreck hunters a mysterious clue
The lost remains of a luxury passenger steamer that sank more than 150 years ago have been discovered at the bottom of Lake Michigan, some 20 miles off the coast of Wisconsin.
Paul Ehorn, 80, a shipwreck hunter and scuba diver, identified the Lac La Belle after more than 60 years of searching. The vessel, known as “one of Lake Michigan’s most sought-after missing ships,” was found with its hull upright and intact, according to a statement from Shipwreck World.
Ehorn, who became a certified scuba diver at the age of 15, now has 15 shipwreck discoveries to his name. But few are more significant to him than the Lac La Belle, which had been his white whale since 1965.
“As a woodworker myself, I appreciate the hand craftsmanship that went into these early vessels,” Ehorn says in the statement. “The Lac La Belle was close to home for me and is a wreck that’s always been on my radar.”
The Lac La Belle disappeared on a stormy night in October 1872 during its usual journey between ports in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, and Grand Haven, Michigan. On this trip, the steamer carried 53 passengers and crew members, 19,000 bushels of barley, 1,200 barrels of flour, 50 barrels of pork and 25 barrels of whiskey, according to the statement.
Roughly two hours into the voyage, the ship sprang a leak, forcing its return to Milwaukee. But the storm worsened, and tall waves soon extinguished the ship’s boiler fires. Winds pushed the vessel south as the captain ordered passengers into lifeboats in the early morning hours. From this precarious position, everyone watched as the Lac La Belle sank stern-first.
The lifeboats made for shore between Kenosha and Racine, but the rains didn’t relent. One lifeboat capsized, and eight people drowned.
Records of the Lac La Belle’s route that fateful night left shipwreck hunters with a large search radius, making the hunt challenging. For years, divers—including Ehorn—searched to no avail. But in 2022, maritime historian Ross Richardson got a tip that cracked the case wide open.
Though Richardson declined to detail the specifics of this clue, he tells the Associated Press’ Todd Richmond that a commercial fisherman at a “certain location” had hooked an artifact that’s specific to 19th-century steamships. With this information, Ehorn was able to narrow his search.
“To find her was really quite an accomplishment. She was in a big grid,” Brendon Baillod, president of the Wisconsin Underwater Archaeology Association, tells FOX6 News’ Jason Calvi. “I’ve got to give it to Paul. I didn’t think that shipwreck would be found, ever.”
Quick facts: Another recent Great Lakes discovery
Using sidescan sonar technology, Ehorn and his partner, Bruce Bittner, surveyed the potential site in October 2022, nearly 150 years to the day of the wreck. In just two hours, the pair was able to identify the 217-foot-long steamer’s unmistakable arched design. Hired scuba divers explored the wreckage, which they noted was in remarkably good shape despite the violent storm that sank it.
“I anticipated searching for a few weeks,” Ehorn tells the Milwaukee Journal Sentinel’s Ricardo Torres. “I got lucky—and had a lot of research.”
Announcement of the discovery was delayed for more than three years because Ehorn wanted to develop a 3D video model of the wreckage. Due to the ship’s hard-to-reach location and poor weather, divers weren’t able to return to the site until last summer, per the AP.
Built in 1864, the Lac La Belle spent its early years traveling from Cleveland ports to Lake Superior before a collision left it submerged in 25 feet of water in the St. Clair River in 1866. After the ship was raised in 1869, it was purchased by Englemann Transportation Company in Milwaukee.
Ehorn will be presenting the discovery in March at the upcoming Ghost Ships Festival, an annual gathering centered around nautical archaeology and maritime history in Manitowoc, Wisconsin.
“It was one more [shipwreck] to put a check mark by,” Ehorn tells the AP. “Now it’s on to the next one. It’s getting harder and harder. The easier ones have been found.”
